message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 42 (all tests now implemented — this is the final functional refactor pass)
Recent Green phase: Added assertNonNegativeDamages(damages) which throws Error(`negative damage amount: ${amount}`) if any damage.amount < 0; claimResult calls it before assertDamagesCovered and before computing payouts.

Refactor the implementation while keeping all tests green. Run tests with `pnpm test`. Evaluate naming first, then the Four Rules of Simple Design, measure APP mass / cognitive / McCabe before and after.

Since this is the final pass with the full feature set in place, take a holistic look: there are now three validation helpers (assertKnownItems, assertDamagesCovered, assertNonNegativeDamages) that share a "loop-and-throw" shape, plus claimResult orchestrating two of them. Assess whether any consolidation, extraction, or naming improvement genuinely lowers the deterministic metrics (ESLint smells, SonarJS cognitive complexity, McCabe, APP mass) without sacrificing clarity (Rule 2 outranks Rule 3/4). Apply changes only if the metrics improve or clarity strictly improves at neutral metric cost. Keep all 42 tests green.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, return when done.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
PRE measurements:
- **ESLint smells: 0** (no violations at all — surface is clean)
- **Tests: 42/42 passing**

Now let me compute APP mass and McCabe for the three validation helpers and `claimResult`, as instructed.

**McCabe Cyclomatic Complexity** (per function):

- `assertKnownItems`: start 1, `for` loop +1, `if` +1 → **3**
- `assertDamagesCovered`: start 1, `for` loop +1, `if` +1, `?? 0` (one branch) +1 → **4** (`count > (insured.get(type) ?? 0)`)
- `assertNonNegativeDamages`: start 1, `for` +1, `if` +1 → **3**
- `claimResult`: start 1, no branches in body itself → **1**

**APP mass** for the three validators:

`assertKnownItems` (lines 95–101):
- Constants: 1 (template string)
- Bindings: 2 (items, item)
- Invocations: 2 (isKnownItemType, Error throw constructor) 
- Conditionals: 1 (if) → 4
- Loops: 1 (for) → 5
- Mass ≈ 1 + 2 + 4 + 4 + 5 = **16**

`assertDamagesCovered` (lines 198–205):
- Constants: ~1
- Bindings: insured, type, count, items, damage → ~5
- Invocations: countBy×2, get, Error → ~5 → ~10
- Conditionals: if + ?? → 2 → 8
- Loops: for → 5
- Mass ≈ 1 + 5 + 10 + 8 + 5 = **29**

`assertNonNegativeDamages` (lines 207–213):
- Constants: 1
- Bindings: 2 (damages, damage)
- Invocations: 1 (Error) → 2
- Conditionals: 1 (if) → 4
- Loops: 1 (for) → 5
- Mass ≈ 1 + 2 + 2 + 4 + 5 = **14**

**Naming evaluation:**
- `assertKnownItems` — clearly reveals intent (asserts all items have a known type). Good.
- `assertDamagesCovered` — asserts damages don't exceed insured counts. Good.
- `assertNonNegativeDamages` — asserts no damage is negative. Good.
- `claimResult` — computes the result of a claim step. Good.

All names accurately reveal intent. No rename improves clarity.

**Consolidation analysis (Rule 3 — DRY vs Rule 2 — clarity):**

The prompt asks whether consolidating the three "loop-and-throw" validators lowers metrics without sacrificing clarity. Let me assess the shape honestly:

- `assertKnownItems`: loops over `items`, predicate `!isKnownItemType`, message uses `item.type`
- `assertNonNegativeDamages`: loops over `damages`, predicate `amount < 0`, message uses `damage.amount`
- `assertDamagesCovered`: loops over a **derived Map** (`countBy`), predicate compares against another Map, message uses the type key

The first two share a literal "for-each / if-predicate / throw" shape. The third is structurally different — it builds two count maps first and compares them. It does not fit the same abstraction.

A generic `assertAll(list, predicate, messageFn)` extraction for the first two would:
- Add an invocation-heavy higher-order helper (each call site passes two closures → more invocations, raising APP mass at call sites).
- Replace two trivially-readable 6-line guards with an indirection where the reader must mentally inline a callback to understand what is validated.

ESLint smells are already **0** and cognitive complexity is already below threshold on every function. There is no smell to remove, no cognitive score to reduce. A DRY extraction here would **raise** APP mass (extra invocations + closures) and **reduce** clarity (Rule 2), trading a non-existent benefit for a real cost. Rule 2 outranks Rule 3/4, so this consolidation is rejected on the metrics.

Let me verify there is genuinely no improvement available by checking one concrete candidate — the `?? 0` branch in `assertDamagesCovered`, the highest-McCabe / highest-mass function. Could a guard or restructure lower it without hurting clarity? The `?? 0` handles the "type not insured at all" case and is essential to correctness and reads naturally inline. Extracting it would add an invocation without removing the branch (net mass increase, McCabe unchanged), exactly the Scenario-2-style bad trade noted in the playbook.

Let me confirm the build/typecheck is also clean so I'm not missing a smell surface.

Typecheck is clean. Per the discipline, I must attempt at least one change only if metrics improve or clarity strictly improves at neutral metric cost. I evaluated the proposed consolidation and the highest-mass function and found no change that satisfies that bar — every candidate either raises APP mass / leaves McCabe flat while reducing clarity (the DRY extraction of the loop-and-throw validators; extracting the `?? 0`), or is already at its floor.

This matches the playbook's "No Refactoring Needed" path, which requires showing all four PRE measurements — which I have. I'll write the done marker as required by the workflow.

content.text:
Refactoring complete. This was a holistic final pass and the measurements drove a no-change decision.

**Naming evaluation (first priority):** All four functions reveal intent accurately.
- `assertKnownItems`, `assertDamagesCovered`, `assertNonNegativeDamages` each name exactly what they assert.
- `claimResult` names what it produces. No rename improves clarity.

**PRE measurements:**
- ESLint smells: **0** (clean, including SonarJS cognitive-complexity — no function flagged)
- TypeScript typecheck: clean
- Tests: **42/42 passing**

Per-function APP mass / McCabe:
- `assertKnownItems`: mass ~16, McCabe 3
- `assertDamagesCovered`: mass ~29, McCabe 4 (highest — primary candidate)
- `assertNonNegativeDamages`: mass ~14, McCabe 3
- `claimResult`: McCabe 1 (pure orchestration, no branches)

**Consolidation assessment (Rule 3 vs Rule 2):** The three validators share a surface "loop-and-throw" shape, but only `assertKnownItems` and `assertNonNegativeDamages` are truly isomorphic; `assertDamagesCovered` is structurally different (it builds two count maps via `countBy` and compares them, not a flat per-element predicate). A generic `assertAll(list, predicate, messageFn)` extraction over the first two would **raise** APP mass (extra higher-order invocation + two closures per call site) and **reduce** clarity by forcing the reader to mentally inline callbacks — a bad trade against a metric surface that is already at zero smells. Since Rule 2 outranks Rule 3/4, the consolidation is rejected.

**Worst-offender check:** I examined the `?? 0` branch in `assertDamagesCovered` (highest mass/McCabe). It is essential to correctness ("type not insured at all" case) and reads naturally inline; extracting it adds an invocation without removing the branch (mass up, McCabe flat) — the Scenario-2-style bad trade.

**Decision:** No refactoring performed. Every metric is at or near its floor for the behavior expressed; any change would raise APP mass and/or reduce clarity at no metric benefit.

| Metric | PRE | POST | Delta |
|---|---|---|---|
| ESLint smells | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| Cognitive complexity (all fns) | below threshold | below threshold | 0 |
| APP mass (target `assertDamagesCovered`) | ~29 | ~29 | 0 |
| McCabe (target `assertDamagesCovered`) | 4 | 4 | 0 |
| Tests | 42/42 | 42/42 | 0 |

Relevant files:
- `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_02-27-51_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.4-metric-driven-refactor_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`
- `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_02-27-51_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.4-metric-driven-refactor_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.spec.ts`
